title: AP faces dual threat of extreme heat & rising ozone pollution, finds IIT study
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds") Andhra Pradesh
full_text:
Visakhapatnam: Heatwaves in
Andhra Pradesh are not only pushing up temperatures but also worsening air quality by increasing surface ozone levels, according to a study by researchers at the Centre for Ocean, River, Atmosphere and Land Sciences (CORAL), Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Kharagpur.
The study found that the East Coast climatic region, which includes coastal Andhra Pradesh, records a noticeable rise in ground-level ozone during heatwaves. Researchers said prolonged hot spells weaken sea-breeze circulation, reducing the inflow of cleaner marine air and trapping pollutants near the surface. Combined with intense sunlight, these conditions accelerate ozone formation.
Ozone levels in the region were found to increase by 14% to 25% above the
World Health Organization’s guideline of 70 micrograms per cubic metre during heatwaves. In major heatwave years, concentrations rose by 5.5 to 7 micrograms per cubic metre due to stagnant atmospheric conditions and weaker sea breezes.
The study noted that rising temperatures, coupled with emissions from vehicles, industries, thermal power plants and open burning, are creating a combined climate and air-pollution threat. Researchers warned that the trend could lead to a rise in respiratory and cardiovascular illnesses.
For Andhra Pradesh, the findings are particularly significant. coastal districts that usually benefit from cooling sea winds may experience disrupted air circulation during prolonged heatwaves, allowing pollutants to accumulate. High temperatures and strong sunlight then accelerate the conversion of nitrogen oxides and volatile organic compounds into ground-level ozone. As a result, cities such as Visakhapatnam, Vijayawada and other coastal urban centres could witness short-term spikes in ozone pollution during extreme weather events.
Published in the journal npj Clean Air, the study was conducted by Parambat Sangeetha and Jayanarayanan Kuttippurath of CORAL, IIT Kharagpur. The researchers said ozone levels often remain elevated even after heatwaves subside, increasing the risk of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and ischaemic heart disease.
Children, senior citizens, outdoor workers and people with pre-existing respiratory conditions are among the most vulnerable groups. The researchers called for integrated policies to address both climate change and air pollution as compound heatwave-pollution events become more frequent.
